prompt:
# MyYogaTeacher Brand Guide

This content is auto-injected into every prompt sent to the image model (see
CLAUDE.md / decisions.md — "Brand guide injection"). Keep it concise and
directive; every line here is tokens spent on every single generation call.

## Brand Name
Always render "MyYogaTeacher" in any visible text — never "MYT" or other
abbreviations. This matches the standing convention across all MYT
customer-facing copy. Keep any on-image text minimal and legible — this
is a supporting visual, not a text-heavy graphic.

## Visual Style
No single house style is mandated — photorealistic, illustrated, or mixed
styles are all valid and should follow whatever the specific campaign's
reference images establish. If a campaign has no reference images, default
to photorealistic, natural-light lifestyle photography (not stock-photo
gloss, not flat illustration).

## Who Should Appear in Images
Primary audience: Indian diaspora abroad, ages 35–65, ~70% women. This is
not a young fitness-influencer audience — most are sedentary desk workers
re-entering movement after years of inactivity, often self-conscious about
their bodies and intimidated by traditional "fitness" imagery.

- Show real, relatable body types — not exclusively lean/athletic models.
  A 45-year-old with a normal, everyday build doing yoga is more on-brand
  than a fitness-model physique.
- Represent a realistic age range — 35–65, not skewed young. Include some
  gray hair, some visible age — this audience should see themselves, not
  an aspirational stranger.
- Prioritize women (~70% of imagery), but include men naturally, not as
  token inclusion.
- Indian and Indian-diaspora representation should look authentic and
  contemporary — practitioners living in the US/UK/Canada/UAE, in normal
  homes, not staged "exotic" studio sets.
- Where natural for the scene, imply guided/personal instruction (e.g. a
  teacher's presence, a device showing a video call, a subtle
  student-and-teacher dynamic) rather than a generic solo-practitioner
  stock-photo vibe. MYT's core product is 1:1 teacher matching, not
  generic yoga content.

## Poses & Practice Level
- Default to beginner/gentle poses — seated stretches, gentle standing
  poses, restorative positions. This audience has NOT practiced
  consistently in years.
- Avoid advanced/intimidating poses (deep backbends, inversions,
  contortion-level flexibility) as default imagery — these alienate the
  exact person we're trying to reach. Reserve advanced poses only if a
  specific campaign is explicitly targeting experienced practitioners.
- Show modifications/props (blocks, cushions, chairs) where natural —
  reinforces "this is accessible to my body."

## Cultural Authenticity
- Avoid orientalist visual clichés — random Sanskrit symbols used
  decoratively, overly staged "guru" framing, exoticized costuming (see
  also "Explicitly Avoid" below).
- If depicting teachers, they should read as genuine, credentialed
  instructors — approachable authority, not performative spirituality.

## Color Palette
Use as accent/wardrobe/prop/background colors where the scene calls for
color, rather than forcing them unnaturally into every image:

- Primary: `#EE731B` (brand orange — use as the standout accent, not a
  dominant fill), `#06A778` (green), `#FFFBF3` (off-white), `#1C0221`
  (near-black/deep plum)
- Secondary: `#CAE9FF` (light blue), `#FBACBE` (pink), `#E8E1EF` (light
  lavender), `#FCF9C5` (light yellow)
- Offset/tint variants: 20% tints of the primary colors above are
  acceptable for soft backgrounds or shadows

Do not force all of these into one image — pick 1-2 that suit the scene
naturally (e.g. a yoga mat, wall accent, clothing detail).

## Settings & Locations
Favor, in rough order of preference:
- Home environments (living rooms, bedrooms, home offices, balconies) —
  strongly preferred over studio settings, since this reflects how MYT is
  actually practiced (live via Zoom, at home), not staged
- Neutral, minimal studio backdrops — acceptable when a campaign needs
  clean space for text overlays, but home settings should be the default
- Outdoor/nature settings (backyard, park, natural light) — aspirational
  but still relatable, not exotic or unattainable

A laptop, tablet, or phone visible in-frame occasionally is fine and
honest — this is how classes are actually delivered, not something to
hide.

Avoid:
- Generic yoga-retreat or gym-class settings unless a campaign
  specifically calls for it
- "Yoga studio stock photo" aesthetics — bamboo backgrounds, zen gardens,
  incense — these read as westernized wellness cliché, not authentic
  Indian practice

## Explicitly Avoid
- Religious or spiritual iconography (deities, religious symbols, temple
  imagery, mala/prayer motifs) — MYT positions yoga as wellness/fitness,
  not a religious practice
- Generic "mystical India" decorative clichés — these are a Western
  projection of Indian culture, not authentic to this audience's actual
  practice
- Stock-photo clichés: beach-at-sunset silhouettes, meditating-on-a-
  mountain-peak, generic zen-garden imagery
- Bikini/swimwear-style yoga imagery — common in Western yoga stock
  photos, not aligned with this audience or brand
- Advanced/intimidating poses as default imagery — see "Poses & Practice
  Level" above
- All-young, all-thin, all-Western casts — see "Who Should Appear in
  Images" above
- Sterile gym or clinical-looking settings
- Any use of "MYT" as visible text — always "MyYogaTeacher" (see "Brand
  Name" above)

## Reference Image Handling
Some campaign reference folders contain multi-pose infographic composites
(pose grids, numbered sequences, "N poses for X" layouts) rather than
single-subject photos/illustrations. Treat these as layout, composition,
and color-palette inspiration only — never copy or trust any visible text
within them (titles, pose labels, captions). Some of these references are
scraped from third-party sources or are themselves AI-generated and may
contain typos or inaccuracies; only text explicitly given in the prompt
(and the brand name convention above) should appear as rendered text in
output.

## Mood & Emotional Tone
Every image should read as: warm, calm, judgment-free, approachable, and
credible — not clinical, not overly aspirational/luxury. Images should
feel like "a real person practicing with real guidance," not an ad for a
fitness supplement or a fitness bootcamp.

- Avoid: intensity, strain, competitive energy, "no pain no gain" framing
- Avoid: clinical/sterile gym aesthetics
- Aim for: soft natural light, relaxed facial expressions, a sense of ease
  and safety — this is a re-entry into movement, not a fitness bootcamp

prompt:
# MyYogaTeacher Brand Guide

This content is auto-injected into every prompt sent to the image model (see
CLAUDE.md / decisions.md — "Brand guide injection"). Keep it concise and
directive; every line here is tokens spent on every single generation call.

## Brand Name
Always render "MyYogaTeacher" in any visible text — never "MYT" or other
abbreviations. This matches the standing convention across all MYT
customer-facing copy. Keep any on-image text minimal and legible — this
is a supporting visual, not a text-heavy graphic.

Never render a logo, icon, or brand mark of any kind (leaf/lotus symbols,
wordmark lockups, etc.) — the model has no knowledge of the real
MyYogaTeacher logo and will fabricate a plausible-looking fake one. If a
scene calls for brand attribution, use plain text only ("MyYogaTeacher"),
never an accompanying icon or stylized logo treatment. Real logo
placement, if ever needed, should be composited in separately, not
generated.

This also covers fabricated branded merchandise or props — do not invent
products (yoga mats, straps, bottles, apparel, packaging, etc.) labeled or
tagged with "MyYogaTeacher" or any brand mark unless the prompt explicitly
asks for MYT-branded merchandise in-scene. Unless branding is specifically
requested, props should be generic/unbranded.

## Visual Style
No single house style is mandated — photorealistic, illustrated, or mixed
styles are all valid and should follow whatever the specific campaign's
reference images establish. If a campaign has no reference images, default
to photorealistic, natural-light lifestyle photography (not stock-photo
gloss, not flat illustration).

## Who Should Appear in Images
Primary audience: Indian diaspora abroad, ages 35–65, ~70% women. This is
not a young fitness-influencer audience — most are sedentary desk workers
re-entering movement after years of inactivity, often self-conscious about
their bodies and intimidated by traditional "fitness" imagery.

- Show real, relatable body types — not exclusively lean/athletic models.
  A 45-year-old with a normal, everyday build doing yoga is more on-brand
  than a fitness-model physique.
- Represent a realistic age range — 35–65, not skewed young. Include some
  gray hair, some visible age — this audience should see themselves, not
  an aspirational stranger.
- Prioritize women (~70% of imagery), but include men naturally, not as
  token inclusion.
- Indian and Indian-diaspora representation should look authentic and
  contemporary — practitioners living in the US/UK/Canada/UAE, in normal
  homes, not staged "exotic" studio sets.
- Where natural for the scene, imply guided/personal instruction (e.g. a
  teacher's presence, a device showing a video call, a subtle
  student-and-teacher dynamic) rather than a generic solo-practitioner
  stock-photo vibe. MYT's core product is 1:1 teacher matching, not
  generic yoga content.

## Poses & Practice Level
- Default to beginner/gentle poses — seated stretches, gentle standing
  poses, restorative positions. This audience has NOT practiced
  consistently in years.
- Avoid advanced/intimidating poses (deep backbends, inversions,
  contortion-level flexibility) as default imagery — these alienate the
  exact person we're trying to reach. Reserve advanced poses only if a
  specific campaign is explicitly targeting experienced practitioners.
- Show modifications/props (blocks, cushions, chairs) where natural —
  reinforces "this is accessible to my body."

## Cultural Authenticity
- Avoid orientalist visual clichés — random Sanskrit symbols used
  decoratively, overly staged "guru" framing, exoticized costuming (see
  also "Explicitly Avoid" below).
- If depicting teachers, they should read as genuine, credentialed
  instructors — approachable authority, not performative spirituality.

## Color Palette
Use as accent/wardrobe/prop/background colors where the scene calls for
color, rather than forcing them unnaturally into every image:

- Primary: `#EE731B` (brand orange — use as the standout accent, not a
  dominant fill), `#06A778` (green), `#FFFBF3` (off-white), `#1C0221`
  (near-black/deep plum)
- Secondary: `#CAE9FF` (light blue), `#FBACBE` (pink), `#E8E1EF` (light
  lavender), `#FCF9C5` (light yellow)
- Offset/tint variants: 20% tints of the primary colors above are
  acceptable for soft backgrounds or shadows

Do not force all of these into one image — pick 1-2 that suit the scene
naturally (e.g. a yoga mat, wall accent, clothing detail).

**Multi-figure or multi-pose compositions** (grids, infographics, sequences
with more than one illustrated figure): use ONE accent color consistently
across every figure's outfit, not alternating between two or more. Mixing
e.g. orange and green outfits across a pose grid reads as visually
inconsistent/uncoordinated rather than as a cohesive branded set.

## Settings & Locations
Favor, in rough order of preference:
- Home environments (living rooms, bedrooms, home offices, balconies) —
  strongly preferred over studio settings, since this reflects how MYT is
  actually practiced (live via Zoom, at home), not staged
- Neutral, minimal studio backdrops — acceptable when a campaign needs
  clean space for text overlays, but home settings should be the default
- Outdoor/nature settings (backyard, park, natural light) — aspirational
  but still relatable, not exotic or unattainable

A laptop, tablet, or phone visible in-frame occasionally is fine and
honest — this is how classes are actually delivered, not something to
hide.

Avoid:
- Generic yoga-retreat or gym-class settings unless a campaign
  specifically calls for it
- "Yoga studio stock photo" aesthetics — bamboo backgrounds, zen gardens,
  incense — these read as westernized wellness cliché, not authentic
  Indian practice

## Explicitly Avoid
- Religious or spiritual iconography (deities, religious symbols, temple
  imagery, mala/prayer motifs) — MYT positions yoga as wellness/fitness,
  not a religious practice
- Generic "mystical India" decorative clichés — these are a Western
  projection of Indian culture, not authentic to this audience's actual
  practice
- Stock-photo clichés: beach-at-sunset silhouettes, meditating-on-a-
  mountain-peak, generic zen-garden imagery
- Bikini/swimwear-style yoga imagery — common in Western yoga stock
  photos, not aligned with this audience or brand
- Advanced/intimidating poses as default imagery — see "Poses & Practice
  Level" above
- All-young, all-thin, all-Western casts — see "Who Should Appear in
  Images" above
- Sterile gym or clinical-looking settings
- Any use of "MYT" as visible text — always "MyYogaTeacher" (see "Brand
  Name" above)

## Reference Image Handling
Some campaign reference folders contain multi-pose infographic composites
(pose grids, numbered sequences, "N poses for X" layouts) rather than
single-subject photos/illustrations. Treat these as layout, composition,
and color-palette inspiration only — never copy or trust any visible text
within them (titles, pose labels, captions). Some of these references are
scraped from third-party sources or are themselves AI-generated and may
contain typos or inaccuracies; only text explicitly given in the prompt
(and the brand name convention above) should appear as rendered text in
output.

## Mood & Emotional Tone
Every image should read as: warm, calm, judgment-free, approachable, and
credible — not clinical, not overly aspirational/luxury. Images should
feel like "a real person practicing with real guidance," not an ad for a
fitness supplement or a fitness bootcamp.

- Avoid: intensity, strain, competitive energy, "no pain no gain" framing
- Avoid: clinical/sterile gym aesthetics
- Aim for: soft natural light, relaxed facial expressions, a sense of ease
  and safety — this is a re-entry into movement, not a fitness bootcamp
